did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a hybrid AI approach for skin lesion classification (benign vs. malignant). It fused handcrafted features extracted using the Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) with deep learning features extracted using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN). These fused features were then classified using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) model, achieving 97.1% accuracy on a dataset of 900 dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a novel hybrid approach fusing handcrafted (GLCM) and deep learning (CNN) features for skin lesion classification. It describes specific methods (image preprocessing, feature extraction, SVM classification), reports original results (97.1% accuracy, 98.1% AUC on 900 images), and includes a confusion matrix and comparison tables. Although published in a conference proceedings (CSASE), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Lesion Detection Using Handcrafted and DL-Based Features Fusion
journal: 2025 International Conference on Computer Science and Software Engineering (CSASE)
brief_summary: This study developed a computer-aided diagnostic system to distinguish between benign and malignant skin lesions using dermoscopic images. The proposed method fused handcrafted texture features extracted via the Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) with deep learning features obtained from Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN). These combined features were classified using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) model after preprocessing steps including enhancement, normalization, and segmentation. The model was evaluated on a dataset of 900 dermoscopic images collected from the ISIC website. The hybrid approach achieved a classification accuracy of 97.1% and an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 98.1%. The results indicate that fusing handcrafted and deep learning features improves diagnostic accuracy compared to using either method alone.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classes used in the classification task. The dataset contained 900 images of benign and malignant tumors. Specific mention of 'benign nevi' appears in the context of a cited study [22] using acral melanoma and benign nevi data, but the primary study classifies benign vs malignant generally.

One of the two binary classes used in the classification task. The abstract states the study distinguishes between benign and malignant tumors. Melanoma is mentioned as the most dangerous type of skin cancer in the introduction and in the context of cited studies [19], [22].

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (benign vs. malignant skin tumors)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on distinguishing between benign and malignant tumors in dermoscopic skin images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign or malignant) using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier on fused handcrafted (GLCM) and deep learning (CNN) features. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (97.1%) and AUC (98.1%), which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset (subset)
datasets_used.description: A subset of dermoscopic skin images collected from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) website, which is described as an excellent resource for machine learning researchers in medical image analysis and skin cancer detection.

use.training: 80% of the dataset (720 images) was used for the training phase.
use.testing: 20% of the dataset (180 images) was used for testing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the performance of the hybrid model based on feature fusion from GLCM and CNN using validation data, demonstrating correct and incorrect classifications across classes.

statistical_methods_used.method: Sensitivity, Specificity, Accuracy, True Positive Rate (TP Rate), True Negative Rate (TN Rate), Area Under the Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the classification performance of different classifiers (RF, DT, KNN, SVM) and feature fusion strategies (GLCM, CNN, Combined) on the dataset.
To analyze the progress and performance of the proposed model against previous skin cancer categorization studies using different datasets and methods.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, AUC, sensitivity, and specificity metrics, and compared against prior studies in a table, but no statistical significance testing was performed or reported.

ISIC Dataset (subset).description: A subset of dermoscopic skin images collected from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) website, containing 900 images of benign and malignant tumors.
ISIC Dataset (subset).notes: The hybrid model using combined GLCM and CNN features with an SVM classifier achieved the highest performance with 97.1% accuracy and 98.1% AUC. The CNN network was trained with a learning rate of 0.0001, 25 epochs, and 35 maximum iterations. The confusion matrix for the hybrid model validation data using SVM is provided in Fig. 7.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid system fusing Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for deep feature extraction and Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) for handcrafted texture features, classified by Support Vector Machine (SVM)

architecture_details.fusion_method: Concatenation of CNN deep features and GLCM handcrafted features

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named (standard SGD/Adam not specified, but learning rate provided)

preprocessing_techniques: Image enhancement using a Gaussian filter
Normalization using histogram normalization method to eliminate brightness variation
Segmentation to distinguish the skin tumor area (ROI) from healthy skin or background
Zero-padding to standardize Region of Interest (ROI) image size to 226x301 pixels
Data augmentation (rotations, flips, color adjustments) to handle dataset imbalance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to handle dataset imbalance by creating modified copies of existing images to increase diversity. Specific transformations included rotations, flips, and color adjustments.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid method, which fuses handcrafted texture features extracted via Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) with deep learning features from Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), effectively distinguishes between benign and malignant skin tumors.
The combined GLCM and CNN features classified by a Support Vector Machine (SVM) achieved the highest performance with 97.1% accuracy and an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 98.1%.

The hybrid model outperformed models using only deep learning features (CNN + SVM: 94.1% accuracy) and only handcrafted features (GLCM + SVM: 89.8% accuracy), demonstrating that feature fusion improves diagnostic accuracy.
Among the tested classifiers (SVM, Random Forest, Decision Tree, KNN), the SVM classifier achieved the highest AUC (98.1%) when used with the fused feature set.
The study utilized a dataset of 900 dermoscopic images from the ISIC website, with 80% used for training and 20% for testing.

The results support the potential of the proposed CAD system for early skin cancer detection and suggest that hybrid techniques are valuable for improving medical classification systems and reducing error rates.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, proprietary subset of 900 images from the ISIC dataset rather than the full public benchmark, limiting generalizability and preventing direct comparison with standard ISIC baselines.

No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the internal 20% test split of the collected dataset, raising concerns about overfitting and lack of robustness to diverse clinical populations.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance differences between the hybrid model and baseline models, relying solely on point estimates.

Data leakage risk exists due to the use of data augmentation (rotations, flips, color adjustments) without explicit confirmation that these augmented samples were excluded from the training set during the 80/20 split or cross-validation, potentially inflating performance metrics.

The CNN architecture is custom-built (16 layers) with unspecified hyperparameters beyond learning rate (0.0001) and epochs (25), making reproducibility difficult compared to using established pre-trained backbones like ResNet or VGG.

The dataset composition (class balance) is not reported; if the 900 images are imbalanced, high accuracy may mask poor performance on the minority class, although sensitivity/specificity are provided.
The study is published in a conference proceeding (CSASE) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may indicate less rigorous scrutiny of methodological details and experimental design.

The comparison with prior works in Table III is flawed because it compares the proposed method against studies using different datasets and feature extraction techniques, making the performance claims difficult to contextualize fairly.
